86.reason: [This line returns immediately when a multi-line comment starts, but the comment may span multiple lines. The code does not handle the case where the comment continues on subsequent lines, causing the rest of the file to be skipped.]

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 1.0 , reason:  [This line returns immediately when a multi-line comment starts, but the comment may span multiple lines. The code does not handle the case where the comment continues on subsequent lines, causing the rest of the file to be skipped.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 1.0 , reason:  The current logic only handles single-line comments or block comments that start and end on the same line. It fails to handle multi-line block comments where '/*' appears on one line and '*/' on a subsequent line. Consequently, the content inside such block comments (like the words in the test case's final comment) is not skipped and is incorrectly parsed as tokens.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 1.0 , reason:  [This causes multi-line comments starting in a previous line to be ignored incorrectly. The current implementation only checks for "/*" and "*/" within the same line, so if a comment starts with "/*" and doesn't end on the same line, the rest of that line is discarded, but subsequent lines are still processed normally instead of being skipped until "*/" is found. However, in this specific test case, the comment is at the end and spans only one line, so this may not be the issue.]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 1.0 , reason:  [This causes multi-line comments starting in a previous line to be ignored incorrectly. The lexer fails to process any content after "/*" in the current line and skips the rest of the file if "*/" doesn't appear in the same line, but the logic doesn't handle multi-line /* ... */ comments properly across lines. However, more critically, in the given test case, the trailing "*/" is on the last line with extra content ("zhe shi ... MULT * DIV /"), and the current logic only checks for "*/" if notation_flag was set earlier in the same line, which it wasn't because "/*" appeared in a prior line. Thus, the content after "*/" on the last line is never processed, but in this specific test case, the "/*" and "*/" are on the same line, so that's not the issue. Actually, looking at the test input, the comment is: "/*\n    zhe shi ... \n*/", meaning "/*" is on second-to-last line and "*/" is on last line. The current code processes each line independently. When processing the line with "/*", it truncates at "/*" and sets notation_flag=true, then returns (skips rest). But notation_flag is local to the method call for that line! So when the next line (with "*/") is processed, notation_flag starts as false again, so the "*/" is treated as normal text. Thus, the words "zhe", "shi", etc., and the trailing "MULT * DIV /" are lexed as tokens instead of being skipped. This is the root cause of the extra tokens in the output.]
